text.title: Solo Pivot Decision Framework Core Rules
text.summary: Opinionated pivot taxonomy (segment / feature / business-model / tech / channel) with quantitative trigger signals, sunk-cost guardrails, and a hard rule for distinguishing pivot from quit for solo SaaS founders.

rule.statement: Pivot type MUST be classified into one of five categories: segment / feature / business-model / tech / channel. 'We pivoted' without category is forbidden.

rationale.source: Eric Ries — Lean Startup pivot taxonomy

rule.rationale: Untyped pivots produce simultaneous multi-axis changes that confound learning.

rule.statement: Pivot consideration MUST cite >=1 quantitative trigger: trailing-30d MRR flat or down, activation <5%, churn >8%/mo, NPS <0.
The decision record MUST explicitly list sunk costs (months invested, code, audience built) and explain why they are NOT used as a continuation argument.
rule.rationale: Implicit sunk-cost reasoning is the dominant continuation bias.

rule.statement: Pivot is only legal when operator has >=8 weeks of personal runway to execute. Pivoting on empty runway is quitting in disguise.
If three consecutive pivots fail to move the trigger metric within 12 weeks each, the operator MUST quit, not pivot a fourth time.

rationale.source: Annie Duke — Quit; First Round — pivot fatigue

rule.statement: If applicability conditions fail (any Skip-If trigger holds — pre-launch (use customer-development); <90 days live; >=2-founder team; profitable product seeking optimization), the agent MUST skip this methodology and route to the named alternative or to the methodology fallback.
rule.rationale: Mis-applying a methodology outside its preconditions degrades output quality and pollutes downstream artefacts; the explicit skip-rule keeps the routing tree honest.
